---
#title: "Drone - Willow - Bumblebee"
lightbox: false
---

::: {.column-page}

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-6}
## The Drone-Willow-Bumblebee Project
In the rugged mountains of Western Norway, two species of willow (*Salix lanata* and *Salix lapponum*) play a quiet but vital role the life of bumblebees. As climate change reshapes mountain landscapes, understanding how these willow shrubs are spreading or retreating is becoming increasingly important. This project combines drone photography, field surveys, and artificial intelligence in a pilot study to attempt to automatically detect and map willow bushes across the Finse area. 

By training a deep learning model to recognize willows from high-resolution aerial images, we should be able to survey vast and remote areas far faster than would ever be possible on foot. Although, gathering training data still involves a lot of [manual identification](willow-identification.qmd) and hiking in the gorgeous Finse mountain region. This research can open the door to large-scale monitoring of these ecologically important shrubs for years to come.

The project is a cross disciplinary pilot study, in collaboration with the [PPF-Alpine](https://mountainsinmotion.w.uib.no/) group and XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX. Last spring and autumn, two initial field campaigns took place. See this brief [overview of some results](finse-willow-report.qmd) to find out more. 

To get oriented, have a look at the [various topics](themes.qmd) we present here. Also, check out the interactive map of the Finse field work area below!
:::

::: {.g-col-6}
![Field work group, July 2025](media/team_drone.JPG)

![Finse research station 'live' [webcam](https://www.finse.uio.no/news/webcam/), updated near-daily some time between 6:00 and 18:00. Can you spot any willow bushes?](https://www.finse.uio.no/news/webcam/dagens.jpg)
:::

:::

:::

::: {.my-5}
:::

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| column: screen-inset
#| message: false

  library(leaflet)
  library(sf)
  library(htmlwidgets)

# Load the shapefiles
  zones <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/squares.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
    st_transform(4326) %>%
    st_zm()
  wires <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/wire.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
    st_transform(4326) %>%
    st_zm()
  fishnet <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/fishnet_100m.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
    st_transform(4326) %>%
    st_zm()
  willows <- st_read("media/field_work_shapefiles/willow_tags.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
    st_transform(4326) %>%
    st_zm()

  greenLeafIcon <- makeIcon(
    iconUrl = "https://leafletjs.com/examples/custom-icons/leaf-green.png",
    iconWidth = 38, iconHeight = 95,
    iconAnchorX = 22, iconAnchorY = 94
  )

  leaflet(options = leafletOptions(scrollWheelZoom = FALSE)) %>%
    addTiles() %>%
    addMarkers(lng=7.5244779006725375, lat=60.59364220037374, popup="Finse Alpine Research Station") %>%
    addPolygons(
      data = zones,
      fillColor = "pink",
      fillOpacity = 0.4,
      color = "blue",
      weight = 2,
      popup = ~paste("Field Zone:", Name, "<br>Area:", round(Shape_Area, 0), "m²")
    ) %>%
    addPolylines(
      data = wires,
      color = "red",
      weight = 2
    ) %>%
    addPolylines(
      data = fishnet,
      color = "gray",
      weight = 1
    ) %>%
    addMarkers(
      data = willows,
      icon = greenLeafIcon,
      options = markerOptions(title = "willow")  # tag them for JS targeting
    ) %>%
    onRender("
      function(el, x) {
        var map = this;
        
        // Collect only the willow leaf markers (skip the research station marker)
        // We identify them by having a custom icon (not the default blue pin)
        var willowMarkers = [];
        map.eachLayer(function(layer) {
          if (layer instanceof L.Marker && layer.options.title === 'willow') {
            willowMarkers.push(layer);
          }
        });

        function updateMarkers() {
          var zoom = map.getZoom();

          // Define which markers to show at each zoom threshold
          // every Nth marker shown: zoom < 14 = every 10th, 14-15 = every 5th, 16-17 = every 2nd, 18+ = all
          var step;
          if (zoom >= 18) {
            step = 1;       // all markers
          } else if (zoom >= 16) {
            step = 3;       // every 2nd
          } else if (zoom >= 14) {
            step = 7;       // every 5th
          } else {
            step = 20;      // every 10th (or hide all if you prefer)
          }

          willowMarkers.forEach(function(marker, index) {
            var el = marker.getElement();
            if (el) {
              el.style.display = (index % step === 0) ? '' : 'none';
            }
          });
        }

        map.on('zoomend', updateMarkers);
        updateMarkers(); // apply on initial load

        // Scroll wheel zoom behaviour
        map.on('click', function() { map.scrollWheelZoom.enable(); });
        map.on('mouseout', function() { map.scrollWheelZoom.disable(); });
      }
    ")
```
Finse research station is at the blue pin. Zones in pink are initial areas where the drone flies and gathers [imaging data](finse-willow-report.qmd). The "leaf"-markers show where each willow bush was [identified, gendered and geotagged](willow-identification.qmd) in the field.
